data.title: FBI ups reward to $100K in Nancy Guthrie search
data.description:
The FBI on Thursday raised its reward for information leading investigators to Nancy Guthrie, the mother of NBC’s “Today” host Savannah Guthrie, to $100,000 amid the agency’s search near Tucson, Ariz.

The agency also revealed new identifying information about a possible suspect in connection with her disappearance, which investigators have ruled was a kidnapping. The information was based on video recovered from Nancy Guthrie’s Nest doorbell camera from the night she disappeared.

“The suspect is described as a male, approximately 5’9” – 5’10” tall, with an average build,” the FBI’s Phoenix office said in a post on the social platform X. “In the video, he is wearing a black, 25-liter ‘Ozark Trail Hiker Pack’ backpack.”

“We hope this updated description will help concentrate the public tips we are receiving,” the post reads. “Since February 1, 2026, the FBI has collected over 13,000 tips from the public related to this case.”

Investigators recovered multiple pairs of black gloves near Nancy Guthrie’s home that will be run through a DNA analysis, local outlet KVOA reported.

Earlier Thursday, Savannah Guthrie posted an old home video on Instagram giving tribute to her mother. The “Today” host has posted on social media several times asking for help in Nancy’s search and called on her kidnappers to show “proof of life.”

“Our lovely mom,” Savannah Guthrie wrote in her latest post attached to the video. “We will never give up on her. Thank you for your prayers and hope.”

Along with the gloves and footage, TMZ founder Harvey Levin told Fox News on Wednesday that he received a “bizarre” email from someone who claims they know the kidnapper. Levin said the emailer tried to contact Savannah Guthrie’s brother and sister “to no avail.”

“And they said they want one bitcoin sent to a bitcoin address that we have confirmed is active,” Levin added. “It’s a real bitcoin address. And as they put it, time is more than relevant. So, we have no idea whether this is real or not, but they are making a demand.”

Levin also told CNN that there was “activity” from the bitcoin address before he went on air.

FBI investigators said they were “conducting an extensive search” of roadways along Arizona’s Catalina Foothills. No suspects have been identified, but FBI Director Kash Patel said they were looking into “persons of interest.”

data.title: Confusion reigns over Trump meeting with governors
data.description:
A series of contradictions and reversals from the White House and National Governors Association (NGA) this week has bred widespread confusion over the fate of the bipartisan group’s annual meeting with the president.

All governors are expected to gather Feb. 19-21 in Washington, D.C., for the NGA’s annual conference, which has traditionally coincided with a meeting with the president and a separate black-tie dinner at the White House.

The weekend has historically provided a unique opportunity for bipartisanship, but that prospect now appears at risk of unraveling as President Trump continues to give mixed signals about whether he plans to exclude Democratic governors from next weekend’s events.

The president seemed to reverse course Wednesday after his initial decision to invite only Republican governors to the White House compelled Oklahoma Gov. Kevin Stitt (R), chair of the NGA, to cancel the event.

“NGA staff was informed that the White House intends to limit invitations to the annual business meeting, scheduled for February 20, to Republican governors only,” Stitt wrote in a letter to governors early Monday. “Because NGA’s mission is to represent all 55 governors, the Association is no longer serving as the facilitator for that event, and it is no longer included in our official program.”

“We cannot allow one divisive action to achieve its goal of dividing us,” he added. “The solution is not to respond in kind, but to rise above and to remain focused on our shared duty to the people we serve.”

Stitt said in the letter that, at that point, no invitations had gone out yet. A person familiar with the NGA winter meeting planning told The Hill that Republican governors received invitations to the meeting later that day.

After talking to the president on Wednesday, Stitt said Trump backed down from his decision to exclude Democrats from the business meeting next week, attributing the apparent snub earlier to a “misunderstanding in scheduling.”

“He was very clear in his communications with me that this is a National Governors Association’s event, and he looks forward to hosting you and hearing from governors across the country. President Trump said this was always his intention, and we have addressed the misunderstanding in scheduling,” Stitt wrote in a subsequent letter to his fellow governors at 2 p.m. Wednesday. 

“I look forward to seeing each of you in Washington DC next week,” Stitt continued.

Around 30 minutes later, all Democratic governors received their invitations to the Friday meeting at the White House, according to a person with knowledge of the planning.

Earlier reporting indicated that all but two Democratic governors — Maryland Gov. Wes Moore and Colorado Gov. Jared Polis — were invited to the separate White House dinner for governors and their families on Feb. 21.

It’s unclear if Stitt discussed the Feb. 21 dinner with Trump during their phone call Wednesday, but Stitt’s follow-up letter to his fellow governors only mentioned the Friday business meeting.

So, when Trump took to social media later Wednesday — appearing to conflate the business meeting and the lavish dinner, as he lashed out at Stitt — some Democrats and political observers grew confused about the president’s intentions.

Referring to Stitt’s Monday letter to governors, Trump wrote on social media later Wednesday afternoon that the NGA chair “incorrectly stated my position on the very exclusive Governors Annual Dinner and Meeting at the White House.”

Trump quoted the letter as saying the White House intended to “limit invitations to the annual business meeting, scheduled for February 20th, to Republican Governors only.” 

“That is false!” Trump added. “The invitations were sent to ALL Governors, other than two, who I feel are not worthy of being there.”

Trump named Polis and Moore as the two governors he excluded from the invite list, apparently conflating the business meeting and the dinner, to which all Democratic governors except Polis and Moore were invited. 

Trump, in his post, railed against several Democratic governors and accused Stitt of being a “RINO,” or “Republican in Name Only.” That left some to question whether the president intended to invite Democrats.

“Following that invitation, the president made his derogatory and disrespectful Truth Social post, and I believe that has led to a lack of clarity amongst everyone involved,” a person involved in the NGA planning told The Hill.

A spokesperson for Moore’s office confirmed the governor, on Wednesday afternoon, received the invitation to the White House business meeting on Feb. 20. The spokesperson said the governor has not been invited to the dinner. Polis’s office did not respond to a request for comment.

Trump, in a subsequent post Thursday morning, again lashed out at Stitt, calling him a “RINO” and issuing a veiled threat to endorse a primary challenger. The president, in this most recent post, only referred to attendance at the dinner, not the business meeting.

“In any event, we will soon have a Governor in Oklahoma who knows how to accurately write a Press Release to the Public, in this case, to state that I invited, not happily, almost all Democrat Governors to the Governor’s Dinner at the White House. Stitt, a wiseguy, knew this, but tried to get some cheap publicity by stating otherwise,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

“Anyway, I’ll see whoever shows up at the White House, the fewer the better!” he added.

Questions remain about how the NGA and the Democratic Governors Association (DGA) will respond. 

An NGA spokesperson told The Hill on Thursday that the “NGA is not currently facilitating the dinner unless all governors are invited” but that the association has not received any updates so far about the dinner. 

In a statement provided by a spokesperson, Stitt did not address the apparent contradiction, nor did he comment on the exclusion of Polis and Moore from the Feb. 21 dinner. He also did not respond to Trump’s harsh criticism.

“I am glad the White House changed course and decided to invite all the governors to the Feb. 20 business meeting,” Stitt said in an emailed statement.

The DGA had earlier issued a statement refusing to attend any of the NGA events at the White House if not every governor was invited. The Democratic group did not respond to questions about whether the governors would attend the Friday meeting now that all Democrats had secured the invite.

“If the reports are true that not all governors are invited to these events, which have historically been productive and bipartisan opportunities for collaboration, we will not be attending the White House dinner this year. Democratic governors remain united and will never stop fighting to protect and make life better for people in our states,” the group wrote in a statement Thursday.

data.title: Venezuela's Delcy Rodríguez: Maduro still 'legitimate president'
data.description:
Venezuela’s acting President Delcy Rodriguez on Thursday said Nicolás Maduro is still the “legitimate president” following his capture and arrest by the Trump administration. 

“Yes, I can tell you that President Nicolás Maduro is the legitimate president. I can, I will tell you this as the lawyer that I am. They are both, President Maduro and Cilia Flores the first lady, are both innocent,” Rodríguez said during a Thursday appearance on NBC News’s “Meet the Press.”

Maduro is currently facing charges for narcoterrorism, drug trafficking and corruption after serving at the helm of Venezuela’s government operations for years. 

He is currently being held in New York and awaiting trial before a judge. The regime leader pleaded not guilty to all charges, despite pressure from the Department of Justice to admit responsibility for years of drug trafficking throughout the Latin American nation.

Officials in both Venezuela and Bolivia have been accused of transporting illegal narcotics internationally with the help of high-ranking political leaders known as the Cartel of the Suns.

Maduro is accused of providing military-grade weapons to the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC) and coordinating drug routes with groups in Honduras.

While awaiting the regime leader’s fate, Rodriguez has led the country in the midst of a transitional period in which the Trump administration is looking to tap into Venezuela’s oil rich land.

Energy Secretary Chris Wright told NBC that the relationship with Rodriguez is off to a “tremendous start.”

“We’ve been dealing with Delcy for five weeks now. It’s been an amazing cooperation. She’s delivered information,” he told NBC News’s Kristen Welker.

“Everything we know so far has turned out to be true. She’s made enormous positive changes, including already changing the hydrocarbon law in the country in the first few weeks. So, I would say that cooperation is off to a tremendous start,” he added.

data.title: RFK Jr. says he 'used to snort cocaine off of toilet seats' on Theo Von podcast
data.description:
Health and Human Services (HHS) Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said during the latest episode of Theo Von’s podcast on Thursday that he once snorted cocaine off of a toilet seat while talking about not fearing germs.

Kennedy, an anti-vaccine activist, appeared on “This Past Weekend” when he made the remark. He reflected on meeting with Von at recovery meetings in the Los Angeles area when he brought up still going to in-person recovery meetings during the COVID-19 pandemic.

“I mean for me, I said this when I came in, ‘I don’t care what happens, I’m going to a meeting everyday,'” Kennedy said. “I’m not scared of a germ, you know. I used to snort cocaine off of toilet seats. I know this disease will kill me. If I don’t treat it, which means going to meetings everyday, it’s just bad for my life. For me, it was survival.”

The secretary, who has been in recovery for over 40 years, called it “heartbreaking” when meetings tried to go virtual rather than in person.

The two moved on to discuss Kennedy’s work at HHS and he reflected on his time working as a lawyer.

Kennedy has been open about his past drug use, which he said started in the years after his father, former Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy, was assassinated in 1968 in Los Angeles after winning the state’s presidential primary for the Democratic nomination.

Kennedy was arrested twice for drug possession, the latter of the two arrests leading him to commit to becoming sober.

His remark on Von’s show garnered some pushback. Brad Woodhouse, the president of Protect Our Care, a health care advocacy group, gave a one-word statement about Kennedy’s comments: “Resign.”

The same organization projected messages on the HHS building in Washington, D.C., last month, accusing Kennedy of “MAKING AMERICA SICKER AGAIN,” with another message reading “MEASLES HQ.” This was after the U.S. met a key condition required to lose its measles elimination status, having been eradicated for over 25 years.

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) said it’s too soon to declare the status over, citing that it must determine the exact chain of transmission.

Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) Administrator Mehmet Oz urged Americans to get vaccinated against the disease.

“Take the vaccine, please,” Oz said Sunday on CNN’s “State of the Union.” “We have a solution for our problem. Not all illnesses are equally dangerous, and not all people are equally susceptible to those illnesses. But measles is one [where] you should get your vaccine.”

data.title: Trump approval on immigration slips among independents: Poll
data.description:
Approval of President Trump’s immigration agenda has seen a decline among independents, with most saying that the administration has overstepped with its deportation agenda, according to a new AP-NORC poll released on Thursday.

Polling found that 23 percent approve of Trump’s handling on immigration, down 14 points since March 2025. Six in 10 independents say the president’s deportations of immigrants living legally in the U.S. has “gone too far,” with 7 in 10 independents also saying Trump has “gone too far” with sending federal immigration enforcement officers to U.S. cities.

Overall, 60 percent of Americans say Trump has “gone too far” with sending federal officers to U.S. cities to carry out his deportation agenda. Thirty-eight percent of respondents say they approve of how Trump has handled immigration.

Independents also largely have a negative view of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) at 66 percent, with 17 percent either holding a “very or somewhat” favorable view of the agency or no opinion at all. Those independents with a favorable view align closer with how Democrats view the agency, with 2 in 10 independents saying they view ICE favorably compared to 1 in 10 Democrats, who 89 percent say they hold a negative view of ICE.

“Having the border shut, that’s OK,” independent Rick Kinnett of Crawfordsville, Indiana, told The Associated Press. “But what Trump is doing with ICE and Homeland Security? You don’t go yanking people out of cars. You don’t go shooting people.”

“I spent eight years in the military,” he added. “This is not what I signed up for. This is not what we’re supposed to do. This is not constitutional.”

Republicans hold the highest approval toward ICE at 71 percent, with almost a quarter of GOP voters saying they have a negative view of the agency.

Pushback against Trump’s deportation agenda escalated last month after two U.S. citizens were killed during federal immigration operations in Minneapolis. Democrats in Congress have called for reforms to ICE and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

White House border czar Tom Homan said Thursday that ICE and other federal law enforcement officials would withdraw from Minnesota, citing safer conditions in the state and a drop in activity from agitators. Elected officials from the North Star State praised the draw down but slammed DHS’s tactics and actions during its operations.

On the same day, Senate Democrats blocked a measure to fund DHS, inching the federal government closer to a partial government shutdown weeks after lawmakers agreed to cast a funding bill aside while DHS funding was part of a larger spending package for several departments and agencies. That also resulted in a partial government shutdown.

The AP-NORC survey was conducted Feb. 5-8 and included 1,156 respondents. The margin of error is 3.9 percentage points.

data.title: Appeals court won't let Trump freeze Gateway tunnel funding, for now
data.description:
A federal appeals court allowed a judge’s order to take effect Thursday that requires the Trump administration to resume funding for the Gateway tunnel project connecting New York and New Jersey. 

By declining to intervene, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 2nd Circuit paved the way for the project to resume, at least briefly, as a lawsuit brought by the two Democratic-led states proceeds. The court will issue another ruling after holding oral arguments later this month. 

“The Trump administration’s legal stay to avoid funding the Gateway Tunnel has expired,” New York Gov. Kathy Hochul (D) wrote on social media. “Donald Trump needs to follow the law and get the project back on track immediately.” 

The Transportation Department halted support in the fall for the $16 billion tunnel project, which builds two new tracks under the Hudson River connecting to Penn Station in New York City. 

The government has said the freeze is part of a review of compliance with federal regulations. President Trump reportedly told Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) last month he would drop his hold if Schumer agreed to rename Penn Station and Washington Dulles International Airport after him. 

Last week, U.S. District Judge Jeannette Vargas ordered the administration to resume funding after the Gateway Development Commission told contractors to stop work until funding resumes.

Vargas, an appointee of former President Biden, agreed with New York and New Jersey that the freeze likely runs afoul of federal regulation and is arbitrary and capricious. 

The government has said the ruling puts it on the hook to immediately disburse about $200 million in payments. It urged the 2nd Circuit to halt the order. 

“The government will be forced to disburse those sums without any obvious mechanism for recovering them later if the government prevails on appeal,” the Justice Department cautioned in court filings. 

Vargas had agreed to pause her ruling until late Thursday afternoon so the appeals court could weigh in first. The judge acknowledged the government raised serious arguments as to whether federal law gives her authority to hear the lawsuit, as the case may need to go before a specialty court that hears government contract disputes.  

Just as her Thursday deadline came, the 2nd Circuit issued a brief order scheduling arguments for the week of Feb. 23. But the court declined to intervene in the meantime.

data.title: McMahon leading delegation to Olympic closing ceremony
data.description:
Education Secretary Linda McMahon will lead a U.S. delegation to the closing ceremony of the Winter Olympics in Italy later this month, the White House announced on Wednesday.

The closing ceremony is set to be held on Feb. 22 at Verona Arena in Verona, Italy, with a grand finale that officials say will “weave together music, art, and the pure spirit of sport” and celebrate “unity and the enduring bonds between athletes and nations.”

Joining McMahon on the trip will be U.S. Ambassador to Italy and San Marino Tilman Fertitta and his spouse, Small Business Administration head Kelly Loeffler, senior Trump adviser Meredith O’Rourke and several other wealthy businesspeople and philanthropists.

Ryan Suter, a professional ice hockey player who competed on Team USA in the 2010 Winter Games and medaled in the 2014 Winter Games, is also part of the delegation.

The 2026 Games have been overshadowed by some U.S. athletes’ discomfort representing the American flag amid a fractured domestic political climate, prompting backlash from President Trump and other administration officials.

Vice President JD Vance, who led the U.S. delegation to the opening ceremony, suggested this week that athletes should keep their political opinions to themselves.

“You’re not there to pop off about politics,” Vance told reporters in comments first reported by USA Today.

The U.S. is currently ranked third in the medal count with 14 total gold, silver and bronze medals through nearly the first week of competition. Norway is atop the standings with seven gold medals, followed by Italy, which has the highest overall count at 17.

data.title: Senate departs with DHS shutdown looming and negotiators at loggerheads
data.description:
The Senate left Washington on Thursday without a deal to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), all but ensuring a shutdown of the agency that could stretch into next week.

The two sides remain at loggerheads over the reforms Democrats are demanding to immigration enforcement practices and negotiators were unable to bridge the sizable gulf.

Democrats on Thursday blocked both a full-year DHS funding bill and another short-term extension of current funding, saying they hadn’t seen enough progress in talks.

Members are not only leaving for the weekend, but for a week-long recess. Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) has indicated he will only call the lawmakers back to Washington if negotiators come to an agreement.

“We don’t have a solution yet,” Thune told reporters, adding that Democrats “have no interest” in funding the department at present.

“Negotiations will continue, and we will see in the course of the next few days how serious they are,” he added.

The first bill Democrats blocked Thursday would have funded DHS for the rest of fiscal year 2026, and at one point had the backing of Democratic and GOP leaders in both chambers. That changed when a Border Patrol agent shot and killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, upending the debate over immigration enforcement. The vote failed 52-47, with Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), voting with Republicans. Thune voted “no” in a procedural maneuver in order to bring it up again in the future. 

The minority party also objected to a two-week measure that was offered by Sen. Katie Britt (R-Ala.) via unanimous consent, similar to what the chamber passed at the end of January to keep the department’s lights on. Sen. Chris Murphy (D-Conn.) objected.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) declared the votes were “a shot across the bow at Republicans.”

“Democrats will not support a blank check for chaos,” he said during a press conference. 

The series of votes also came after top Democrats and the White House engaged in their latest horse-trading. The White House on Wednesday sent proposed legislative text, which Schumer on Thursday said was insufficient.

“Their proposal’s not serious, plain and simple,” he said. “It’s very far apart from what we need.”

Democrats have centered the negotiations on tightening of warrant requirements, unmasking agents who are engaged in immigration operations and ending roving patrols in cities and communities. 

All have appeared to be bright red lines for Republicans, with few showing signs of being willing to yield on the three issues. 

Nevertheless, Thune has insisted that talks are progressing. He also argued that border czar Tom Homan’s announcement that enhanced ICE operations in Minneapolis are ending is “additive to the attempts to try to find a solution here.”

“I think the White House is giving more and more ground on some of these key issues,” the GOP leader said, adding that GOP negotiators have “moved a good long way from where they started.”

For now, the timeline for talks is fluid. Thune allowed lawmakers to leave town following the vote on Thursday afternoon, arguing that it was best to let them go until talks get down to the final stages. 

More than a dozen senators are scheduled to attend the Munich Security Conference in the coming days, with others quick to note that next week is a rare recess week that allows them time to attend events across their home states. Senators did not get as many in-state weeks as usual last year given the intense work schedule in Washington. 

Thune, however, said that members could be called back with as little as 24 hours of notice.

While the center of the fight has been over ICE, the brunt of impact will be felt by others across the massive department, especially as the immigration enforcement arm is funded for the foreseeable future due to funds that were green-lighted in the GOP’s “big, beautiful bill.” 

Republicans have been quick to note that no deal means disrupted operations and potential missed paychecks for employees at the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), Transportation Security Administration (TSA) and the Coast Guard, among others.

When asked about the impact on those workers, Schumer pointed the finger across the aisle. 

“Talk to the Republicans,” the Democratic leader told reporters. “They’re the ones holding this up.”

Despite the impasse, some key lawmakers see a pathway to a deal.

Sen. Angus King (Maine), an independent who caucuses with the Democrats and who helped negotiate a deal to end the 43-day shutdown in November, told reporters that despite what appears to be a bleak state of affairs, the mere trading of paper between the two sides indicates they are both truly in search of a deal. 

He labeled the back-and-forth “serious discussions.” 

“As of last night, the White House proposal is not adequate,” King said, noting that he’d been in touch with Schumer’s office. “But it does reflect that the issue is being taken seriously.”

data.title: Newsmax host rips Trump's DOJ over Epstein redactions: 'I want somebody to go to jail'
data.description:
A Newsmax host on Wednesday lambasted President Trump’s Department of Justice (DOJ) over its redactions throughout the files connected to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, calling for “somebody to go to jail.”

Carl Higbie, host of “Carl Higbie Frontline,” said the Trump administration is in engaged in a cover-up by redacting names and identifying information in the files. By law, names were to be redacted either to hide the names of victims and survivors or to obscure the names of individuals under investigation.

“This is either a cover-up or it’s incompetence, and I want somebody to go to jail for it,” Higbie said. “At a minimum, anybody involved with screwing this up should no longer work at the DOJ or have a law license or anything.”

He placed blame on Trump’s first-term former Attorney General Bill Barr and on the Bush, Obama and Biden administrations for having “failed” to release the DOJ’s files on Epstein, saying to Attorney General Pam Bondi it is now “on your plate.”

“You took that in by taking this job,” Higbie said to the attorney general.

Higbie pointed to redactions raised by Rep. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) during Wednesday’s explosive hearing with Bondi. Massie said that hundreds of pages that included FBI interviews were redacted, prompting Higbie to ask why “are hundreds of them fully redacted?”

The Newsmax host praised Rep. Jerry Nadler (D-N.Y.) for asking Bondi if any Epstein co-conspirators or associated individuals were under investigation. Bondi refused to answer, telling Nadler that his “theatrics are ridiculous.”

“As much as I hate Democrats, it was a good question, and the answer is zero,” Higbie said. “This should not be a partisan issue.”

He added that Bondi “should have just shown up with, like, a list of people, like, ‘Hey, here’s who we’re charging, today.'”

“The bottom line is that no one new was in jail,” Higbie later said. “The DOJ has six million pages of investigations and hundreds of victims and no arrests.”

Bondi engaged with Massie, Nadler and other Democratic lawmakers on the House Judiciary Committee over their questions about the Epstein files. She lobbed insults at lawmakers, citing specific crimes in their districts or boasting about the performance of the stock market.

The attorney general at one point accused Rep. Rebecca Balint (D-Vt.) of voting against a resolution against antisemitism, drawing outrage from Balint.

“You want to go there? Are you serious?” Balint said before storming out of the room. “Talking about antisemitism to a woman who lost her grandfather in the Holocaust.”

Bondi asked Republicans on the committee to use some of their time to respond, often detailing crimes in Democratic districts.

data.title: Culver City mayor responds to Bondi crime claims during heated exchange
data.description:
The mayor of Culver City, Calif., clapped back at claims made by Attorney General Pam Bondi about the city’s crime rates.

During a back-and-forth with Rep. Sydney Kamlager-Dove (D-Calif.) about right-wing extremism at a heated congressional hearing on Wednesday, Bondi accused the congresswoman of “not talking about any crime in her district.” Kamlager-Dove represents a district that includes Culver City.

“Nothing about helping crime in her district,” the attorney general said. “She’s not even worth getting into details.”

The city’s mayor, Democrat Freddy Puza, pushed back on these claims in a Wednesday post on Instagram. 

“Let’s be clear about the facts: crime in Culver City declined 9.7% in 2024 and is down another 6.1% in the third quarter of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024,” the mayor wrote. “The full 2025 year-end crime report will be released in the coming weeks.

“Our City is in Congresswoman @rep_kamlagerdove district, and the facts simply do not support the Attorney General’s narrative from today’s Congressional hearing,” he added. “We welcome the correction from the federal Department of Justice, but we won’t hold our breath.”

Kamlager-Dove also responded to the attorney general’s comments in a post on the social platform X after the hearing. 

“Culver City is known for parks, studio lots, and breakfast burritos—not crime,” Kamlager-Dove said. “Pam Bondi should visit sometime.”

Bondi’s Wednesday testimony was punctuated by several other clashes with Democratic lawmakers. 

The attorney general lashed out at members over questions about the Justice Department’s handling of the investigation into convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. 

Bondi got into a shouting match with Rep. Pramila Jayapal (D-Wash.) after the representative asked Bondi to turn around and face 11 victims of Epstein who were in the audience and who said they had not yet been able to meet with the Justice Department. Jayapal asked Bondi to “apologize for what your Department of Justice has put them through.”

Bondi refused, saying she would not “get in the gutter with these theatrics.”
